title: Cerasus lusitanica
intro: Cerasus lusitanica, also known as the wild cherry tree, is a deciduous shrub or small tree native to the Iberian Peninsula and southwestern Europe. The bark and leaves of Cerasus lusitanica have been traditionally used in herbal medicine to treat various health conditions, including fever, cough, and digestive issues, due to its anti-inflammatory and antibacterial properties. The bioactive constituents of this plant include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and saponins, which are responsible for its medicinal effects. The most common herbal preparations made with Cerasus lusitanica include teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are often used to treat respiratory and gastrointestinal issues.
taxonomy: Cerasus lusitanica is a species of flowering plant in the family Rosaceae. It is a type of cherry, specifically a wild cherry native to the Iberian Peninsula. The classification of Cerasus lusitanica is within the subgenus Cerasus, which comprises the cherry species. The subgenus Cerasus is further grouped under the genus Cerasus, and the family Rosaceae. Within the kingdom Plantae, the species is classified as a vascular plant.

preparations: Cerasus lusitanica, also known as the Portuguese cherry, has been used in traditional herbal preparations due to its potential medicinal properties. The plant's leaves, bark, and fruits are often used to make teas, infusions, and tinctures. These herbal preparations are said to have anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant effects. In some cultures, the Portuguese cherry is believed to have a calming effect and is used to treat anxiety and insomnia. However, more research is needed to confirm the efficacy and safety of these herbal preparations.
